sentences.text: Well, I think that these were the clothes that I still had from back-- back way.
I mean, from way back.
Because I mean, you didn't buy anything.
You didn't-- you couldn't-- you couldn't buy anything.
All I remember-- it's interesting.
I was playing-- oh, you were asking me about synagogue.
OK, now I just remember, yeah.
I remember it was Saturday.
And Saturday, you know, when the Germans were not around, whatever, I guess people were observing.
And I remember there were some-- two boys.
And one of them had a new-- a new suit that the parent-- he put on.
It was for Saturday.
And after-- I don't remember whether there was a synagogue and people went to synagogue.
That, I don't remember, but maybe.
Because afterwards, we started all playing.
And this boy somehow ended up on the fence.
You know, he caught-- he was caught on the fence.
And he tore that suit.
And I remember, that was such a tragedy at that time.
That was really-- you couldn't even imagine.
Because here, there was this new suit.
And it was torn.
And there was no one to be gotten again.
So that was an incident that all of a sudden just pop up to my-- popped in my mind.
Yeah, so there was a way of releasing some tension by playing and--
Yes.
Well, for the children.
I don't think there was for the grownups.
The grownups probably were getting together and talking about the situation more and trying to make out-- something out of it and be able to get some food on the table, really.
I think they were Ukrainians.
Ukrainians.
How did you get out?
How did-- how were you able to get through the gates, or the barbed wire?
Well, I guess in-- maybe in some parts of it, maybe it-- maybe there was just wooden fence.
And maybe, you know, somebody broke out the-- what do you call these?
Pieces of the wood.
The slats.
Yeah, somebody probably broke out the slats.
And that's how-- that's how people escaped.
Well, it was quite-- it was quite deep.
It had to be, because people could stand.
Because we had-- there was no room to sit down there.
And whatever on the outside, it looked really like a dog house.
Yeah, it was like a cone with-- with an opening, with a door, which was like a-- like a circ-- like a semicircle on top.
And it had to be quite deep, because otherwise, you know, grownups couldn't stand there.
So maybe it was two by four or maybe smaller.
Maybe two by two.
I don't-- I mean, I see it in front of me, but I can't--
I can't describe.
Yeah, because this was all-- all around it.
It was really like a farm-- farmland, really.
There were small villages with houses on quite-- you know.
Yes.
Oh, you mean in the cellar?
No.
Because what had happened-- that was-- I think that was the end of the summer, probably.
Because otherwise, we could not have walked through the river, because it would have been frozen.
And we walked in the water.
I remember that.
So it had to be end of the summer.
And usually, the potatoes-- they would probably have it only in the fall.
So we were lucky that it happened when it happened.
There were some other Jews there.
No.
There were not a lot.
There maybe were five people, six people.
Probably yes.
I mean, I don't remember knowing them.
But probably yes.
We probably did.
Because the ghetto wasn't that big, really.
So I guess everybody knew everybody.
Um, good question, that they were not afraid that somebody's going to scream, or yell, or cry, or whatever.
I don't remember that.
I don't remember.
Oh, we were told to be quiet, absolutely.
Absolutely.
And then another maybe two or three people joined us.
That was it.
That was it.
And we were petrified.
When-- you know, when the door opened, we thought this was it.
We thought the Germans were there.
Because the Germans were walking with dogs, back and forth, and passing back and forth.
So although we thought these dogs were so well trained, but I guess they were not that well trained.
Because otherwise, they could have probably smelled us.
Well, we were-- my mother was very young at that time.
So I'm sure that were-- I don't remember who they were, really.
But I think they were all younger people.
The older people would not have-- probably did not escape at all.
They were all probably gone.
Yeah.
Well, some of the people that were there.
Yeah, because, I guess, they were young and they were not scared.
And they-- maybe they knew the area a little bit.
Maybe they-- maybe they were swimming in there, in the river.
Because that was, you know, before then, before the war.
Or maybe they were from the area.
And they knew.
And they-- they really walked us, or led us, into the forest.
I was-- that was 1943.
Yeah.
That was-- right.
My sister was five years younger.
Five and a half years younger.
Well, I think somebody must have pushed me up, you know in the cellar, and somebody had to pull me up.
So I think two people were helping me out.
And then we got to the Bug.
And as I said--
Yes.
Yes.
We all left at the same time.
And when we got to the Bug, oh, I remember the water in front of us.
And you know, my mother didn't know how to swim.
Well, I was a young girl.
I had no-- I didn't know how to swim.
My sister was a baby.
So we were a little-- we were very nervous, because we didn't know how we were going across that Bug.
But as I say, it had to be-- it probably was not that deep.
So but I think that my mother carried my sister.
And me carried some-- some young man, I think, carried me.
Because otherwise, I would have never crossed the Bug.
No, because the Germans were very scared of forests.
So the Germans-- that's how we survived altogether, because they were very nervous.
They were scared to get into the forests.
They were scared of the partisans.
And they were scared that they were going to be killed.
So they-- they did not get into those forests.
Well, we started-- I don't remember exactly where was the first stop and whatever.
But they were certain-- first, I think the people built like a chalet-- I mean, chalet.
It wasn't a chalet in a whatever.
They took [INAUDIBLE].
And how did they do it?
I don't know, whether they found dead-- dead, you know, stamps-- I mean, trees, and they could break them.
Because I don't even remember.
I mean, I don't know where they got the-- a saw or some tools to build it.
But they built like a some kind of a chalet?
Not a chalet.
With an open-- you know, open on the top.
And just put, like, the woods, you know, like one next to the other.
And we slept on the-- we slept on the ground.
And we had a fire in the middle.
And the smoke was just going out there.
And how many people stayed in each one of these?
I think all of us that-- all of us that-- so maybe it was 10 people.
Not the whole time, but part of the time we stayed together.
Well, maybe to go and pick berries, because, you know, there were berries and there were cranberries in the forest.
So I was going out and picking berries and picking woods for the fire.
Well, eventually we learned how far we could go and how far we couldn't.
We had no watches.
We had no idea of time at all.
But so what I learned-- I learned to look at the sun.
And I knew, well, if the sun was here, then it was midday, or it was in the afternoon, or it was whatever.
So that's how-- that's how we were going.
Later on, I guess, the older people discovered the villages around-- around that area.
And they started venturing out at night to those villages.
And they started to steal potatoes from the fields.
And we came back and we baked those potatoes.
Or they started stealing some other things.
When they found out that there were no Germans in the area, then they would start going in and knocking at the peasants' houses and beg for food.
And some people were nice and they gave food.
And some people said, go away, Jew, because I'm going to call the German on you.
Well, my mother used to do it in the beginning.
And later on, she wanted me to go.
I just couldn't do it.
I just couldn't do it in the beginning.
No, she went-- she went alone.
She went alone.
She was-- as I said, she was a good looking woman, young.
So-- and I guess some of the people felt sorry for her, or liked her, or whatever it is.
And they gave her food.
So sometimes she would come with bread and sometimes with cheese.
Well, when she went to the houses, she went during the day.
Because at night, nobody would open the doors.
Well, sure, absolutely.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.

Well, during some of these-- of these times, and exactly I don't remember in which period of time, my mother, by probably going to the village and developing some kind-- some kind of a relation with the people, told them about me.
And they had a blind child.
And I guess they were both peasants.
They both had to work in the fields.
And they needed somebody to stay with the blind child and be kind of a nanny to that child.
And my mother gave me-- sent me there.
They took me in.
And I think for three or four months, I was a nanny to this blind child.
I lived there.
No.
I lived-- I lived there.
And that was already, considering living in the forests and whatever it is, this was a big luxury.
So I was helping them.
And I was-- I was a maid and taking care of this-- of this child.
They liked me.
They were nice to me.
No, I don't remember.

And I guess they probably-- either some neighbors discovered that they were having a Jewish girl, because that was happening quite often, or they heard maybe the Germans were coming, or whatever it is.
So they send me-- they send me back.
Now, my sister, on the other hand-- my mother again found some-- found some Poles or Ukrainians, too, and again developed a relationship with them.
And they took my sister in.
And they said, OK, they are going to tell the neighbors that she's a cousin who came-- whose parents passed away, or whatever.
And she came to live with them from whatever.
My sister was dark.
And she was with them for maybe a couple of weeks or a month.
And she developed sickness that you get like-- it's contagious.
And I don't know how it's in English.
It's very contagious and it's very-- it's like pimples all over.
And you know-- and it was infectious disease, really.
And so when they disc-- when they found that-- when they found that she was sick, they somehow got in touch with my mother.
And they told my mother to take her.
So my mother went and picked her up.
And she was walking through the village.
And there was a German.
And all of the southern, a German comes over to her and says, [GERMAN]?
And my sister, at that moment, says, 'Mama.' When they heard that she spoke Polish, they let them go.
So that was one of very close calls.
Maybe my mother did come and visit.
I don't remember.
I really don't remember.
Maybe I got some food from them and I passed on to my mother.
It is possible, but I don't remember.
Well, you know, children didn't have their own rooms there.
There was one big room.
There was a big oven, like a baker's oven, where you bake bread-- this type of an oven-- clay ovens, they were.
And near the ovens, there was a place that you could sleep.
It was really part of the oven.
And that was a warm part of the oven.
So everybody tried to sleep on that oven, really, because that was the warmest part.
But I didn't.
I think I slept in-- you know, in one of the rooms, on a piece of-- you know, some kind of a-- like a bed made out of wood or, you know, whatever-- something like that.
No, I think that they had that only child.
Then, in another part, my mother gave me to-- to other peasants.
And I was working as a cowgirl.
And I had to take out the cows to-- you know, to the pasture and spend the whole day with the cows.
And I had to take them out in the morning and then bring them back at night.
And they were treating me nice.
At least I had butter, and I had milk, and I had foot to eat.
And I stayed with them as well, right.
And I remember that I used to sleepwalk at night.
And very often, I would find myself outside the house, just-- and then they would, I guess, hear whatever.
They would bring me back in.
So during the day-- I mean, while I was on the pasture, there was their son, too, or somebody else.
So you know, we were playing.
And we were in the-- picking whatever there was to pick.
And that was another part of that-- of that ordeal.
My sister was mostly with my mother.
Then, we were-- after-- after living in this-- in those huts or whatever-- hut, really, not chalet-- we would go to-- we would, again, have to move.
Because somebody would say that Germans are close by.
So we would move.
And my mother would, again, develop some relation with some Poles.
And they would let us stay on the-- over the-- over a barn, in-- you know, in straw upstairs.
So we would be laying there the whole day, not be able to move.
Because--
And my sister.
No, just me and my mother and my sister.
And I don't remember if my uncle was-- whether he was with us or not at that time.
So we were hiding there.
And at night, they would bring us some food.
And when we had to go to the bathroom, it was going at night.
We had to hold the whole day.
We couldn't-- couldn't walk down from there.
And again, we heard people coming, and neighbors, and whatever.
And everybody was snooping because whatever.
Then, again, when we were traveling from one village to another, from one place of the forest to another, we would end up in somebody's field and sleep in a hay-- in a big hay--
--haystack.
You know, my mother would go in and just make-- like, pull out something.
And the hay is very warm.
So we could survive.
And in the middle of the night, we would hear dogs barking.
And we would hear somebody would say, oh my god.
What's happening now?
Why that the dog is barking now?
Well, maybe the Germans are around the corner-- and you know, things like that.
Yeah.
Yes, but it would have been, again.
But these forests were quite thick.
And the forests were quite big.
And we were not exactly next to a village.
We were really in the middle of a forest.
There probably were.
There probably were.
No.
No.
Not at that time, no.
Maybe later on, maybe.
But not-- not at that-- that was really the initial time.
Because there was a time when we ended up in [NON-ENGLISH], there, where we were already, or not far from there.
And again, we somehow-- we were in the forest.
And my mother, again, went to town.
And some Poles gave her a little room.
My uncle was with us at that time.
And the Pole that gave us this little apartment, or this little room-- whatever-- maybe my mother did have some money at that time.
Maybe she did have.
Yes, she did have.
Because I remember, she used to wear like a corset.
And in that corset, in the pocket, she did have some money.
So in some instances--
I guess-- I guess she must have taken it from [NON-ENGLISH].
Maybe she always wore that corset, because she was afraid probably.
She was afraid to leave it.
There were so many people, that somebody will steal it.
She-- I think she always wore it, now when I think about it.
So it was like part of her, really.
When she got up, when she got dressed in the morning, that's what she wore.
And the money was sewed in there.
So I remember this incident.
She was-- so he gave us this little apartment.
And his parents lived in the same house, or the same-- next house.
He was married.
And he wanted my mother.
You know, he said, I'll leave you here.
I won't tell them anything.
But you have to have sex with me.
And my mother was avoiding it by giving all kinds of excuses, really.
She can't.
She's not-- da, da, da, da, da, until it got to a point where he finally kicked us out from there.
Because he couldn't get it, he kicked us out.
And where did you go?
I-- before we end this, I don't remember whether that was in this particular place, where I developed rheumatic fever.
My uncle got typhoid.
And then, later on, my mother got typhoid.
I mean, thank God both of them survived.
The typhoid, I guess, was not that severe.
Now, I with that-- with my rheumatic fever, I was sick for about half a year.
I walked out, outside, because there was-- as I said, there was no plumbing.
There was nothing.
I went outside.
And I had to release myself, or I fell.
I couldn't get up.
That was it.
For half a year, I couldn't walk.
I couldn't walk.
And I couldn't-- I was incapacitated.
I couldn't do anything with my hands and I couldn't walk.
So I stayed in bed for about half a year.
At that time, I think we were in this guy's house.
But what had happened, that at that time, my mother had developed somehow-- there was a priest, with whom my parents-- my mother, not my-- my father was not alive anymore.
My mother developed, you know, some kind of a relationship because she started begging, I think.
When she was begging, she went.
She knocked at this priest's house.
--the housekeeper was very nice.
And she was giving my mother food.
And then the priest found out about us, or learned about us.
And he was a human being.
And he started helping us.
And being, I guess, that he developed good relation with us, the people in the village somehow tolerated us.
I don't know if everybody knew, or who knew, or who whatever.
But he was helping us.
And he sent us a Polish doctor who was in that village.
And this doctor was coming, and visiting with me, and was helping my mother during the typhoid, and my uncle during the typhoid, and me with-- with this rheumatic fever.
He was giving me some kind of a medication.
I don't know what it was.
And after half a year, I started to learn how to hold a cup in my hand again.
It was like completely new experience.
And I remember, when I made my first step after that, I was like a baby who was-- you know, who is 18 months old and starts to walk.
Somebody had to stand in front of me.
And I was making steps and running to that person.
The person would be catching me.
And that's how finally I got back on my feet.
I don't remember which house we were in, but somewhere, in somebody's house.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
Maybe we did.
Well, what had happened is that when we were in these villages, every so often, you know, the Poles would come to us, that we were staying with or that were helping us-- whatever.
And they would say, I think the Germans-- the Germans are coming.
So when that was, we had to run to the forest.
And at that time, some other group of Jews-- or maybe that was the one that we were, you know, in contact with.
I don't really remember.
They had built already a winter structure.
Because sometimes we had to run away.
And that was one time that I had with my legs that I couldn't walk, that we had to run away there, because they were afraid that if the Germans come to the village, everybody's going to be killed.
So again, this, the winter structure, was built in the ground.
Oh, it had to be-- you know, it had to be for a person to practically stand up.
Maybe a little--
Again, it was maybe for 20 people.
And again, there was an opening on the top for the fire, because that was the only way for us to keep warm.
And you slept-- they built from the-- from a trees-- you know, like from 10 trees, they built like kind of a benches or whatever that you slept on.
And a few times, we had to-- we ended up there-- to sit through that scary part of time, when they were telling us the Germans are coming into the villages.
How did they find out?
How did they know about it?
I don't know.
But that was happening.
Were you with the original group all the time?
I don't remember that.
I don't think so.
I don't think so.
Groups?
In the villages.
I don't remember any children in those-- in the house that we stayed.
But in the villages, where we stayed, I guess there were some other families that were hidden.
And I remember particular about a girl who I was friendly with-- again, I don't remember anybody's name.
It's terrible.
And she was hidden under a down quilt.
But the quilts are not like quilts that we know them today.
There was like a big, you know, thick-- maybe this-- this thick.
And this child was there the whole day, under that quilt, with the bed all made up, without-- I don't know how she-- how she was getting air.
But I meant in the actual woods, in the huts and the underground thing--
Yeah, I don't remember.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
Well, maybe-- you know, they probably were stealing them from the-- you know, from the peasants.
Because I mean, nothing was really locked.
I mean, and these peasants lived on pieces-- big pieces of, you know, properties.
And everything was done with a shovel and very primitive-- you know, with the saw, and the shovel, and whatever.
They were no combines.
There were no tractors.
There were nothing.
Well, I remember one was, you know-- one was this [NON-ENGLISH] that we were hidden.
One was [NON-ENGLISH], where we moved after already, after the war.
I don't remember the others, no.
I don't.
Boy, I didn't think that that's--
